perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its principles of conflict of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Arbitration shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its principles of conflict of laws.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the choice of law clause to arbitration alters its impact and enforceability. Standard choice of law clauses should apply to the entire agreement, not just arbitration. It may cause confusion and be misinterpreted, thereby hurting enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification attempts to limit the choice of law only to the arbitration clause, which may contradict the broader understanding that a choice of law clause applies to the entire contract. It introduces ambiguity around which state's law governs the other aspects of the contract. The choice of law is crucial for determining how other provisions should be interpreted.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulated that the entire agreement would be governed by Delaware law. The changed text limits the application of Delaware law specifically to arbitration, creating ambiguity as to which law governs the remaining parts of the contract. This structural change introduces a legal contradiction as it potentially undermines the enforceability of the entire agreement, because it is unclear what law applies to aspects of the agreement outside of arbitration. While the scraped snippets discuss the validity and enforcement of arbitration agreements (9 USC 2), they do not address whether a choice of law provision can be limited only to arbitration, or whether the choice of law provision needs to be broad.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior or contemporaneous communications and proposals, whether oral or written, between the parties with respect to such subject matter.
perturbation.changed_text: Arbitration is the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior or contemporaneous communications and proposals, whether oral or written, between the parties with respect to such subject matter.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the entire agreement clause to arbitration creates confusion by suggesting that only arbitration terms are binding. The whole agreement between two parties must be taken in consideration, not just one single element.
perturbation.law_explanation: The changed clause contradicts the principle of integrated agreements by unduly narrowing the scope of the 'entire agreement' to only arbitration. This violates the notion that a fully integrated agreement should represent the parties' complete and exclusive agreement on all terms, not merely the dispute resolution terms. This also affects parol evidence admissibility.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a structural flaw that results in a legal contradiction. By stating "Arbitration is the entire agreement...", it contradicts the established legal principle that the entire agreement encompasses all terms agreed upon by the parties, not just arbitration-related terms. This contradicts the intention of an integration clause, making it more difficult to enforce the contract. Snippet 1 is missing and Snippet 2 is irrelevant. The Perturbation Explanation and the Law Explanation support the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: No waiver of any provision of this Agreement shall be effective unless made in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought. The failure of any party to exercise or enforce any right or provision of this Agreement shall not constitute a waiver of such right or provision.
perturbation.changed_text: Arbitration is the only way that the waiver of any provision of this Agreement shall be effective unless made in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought. The failure of any party to exercise or enforce any right or provision of this Agreement shall not constitute a waiver of such right or provision.
perturbation.explanation: By inserting 'Arbitration is the only way that' at the beginning of this section, enforceability of waiver provisions is compromised. A standard contract would permit a waiver outside of Arbitration.
perturbation.law_explanation: The changed text implies that waivers can only be effective through arbitration, conflicting with general Delaware contract law principles. Under Delaware law, a waiver can be effective through various means, depending on the agreement of the parties. Modifying the waiver clause by limiting its effectiveness solely to arbitration introduces an unnecessary restriction that may not be upheld under Delaware contract law.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for waivers of contract provisions if they are in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought. The changed text adds the requirement that 'Arbitration is the only way' for a waiver to be effective, which contradicts the generally accepted freedom of contract parties to agree on various methods for waiving provisions. The Law Explanation states that under Delaware law, waivers can be effective through various means, depending on the agreement of the parties. Therefore, by restricting waivers to only arbitration, the changed text introduces an unnecessary restriction that may not be upheld under contract law.
